confirmations.reason: Workspace Rules are stored in .agents/rules at the workspace or git root, directly serving as a repo/project-scoped instruction file giving the agent standing instructions and context.

Plugins are described as namespaced bundles grouping skills, rules, MCP servers, and hooks into a single package — directly matching the capability of bundling reusable capabilities/instructions the agent can load.
Lifecycle Hooks explicitly run custom local shell scripts or commands at specific lifecycle points during execution, directly realizing the capability of running custom logic on agent events.

Rules provide persistent, reusable constraints/context but are not described as shaping the system prompt, persona, or output style specifically. The capability targets system-prompt and output customization; rules are closer to behavioral constraints, not output/persona shaping.
MCP Integration directly supports the Model Context Protocol to connect external tools, databases, and services — a direct realization of the external tool/connector protocol capability.
MCP Store is a built-in store to browse, install, and authenticate MCP servers directly in the editor, directly covering the install, configure, and discover aspects of MCP server management.
MCP Store explicitly includes authentication of supported MCP servers, directly realizing the MCP authentication capability.
Per-Tool MCP Permission grants agent access to a single named tool on a specific MCP server, directly realizing fine-grained allow-listing/governance of which MCP tools the agent may use.

Subagents feature explicitly describes delegation of long-running operations to parallel autonomous subagents, directly matching the capability of spawning focused sub-agents to handle parts of a task.

Multi-Agent Orchestration is an advanced framework coordinating multiple agents with error recovery, retries, and coordination logic, directly realizing the capability of multi-agent teams that share work and communicate.
Workflows are saved markdown files defining a repeatable series of steps to guide the agent through tasks, directly matching the capability of defining repeatable, named multi-step task sequences.

Request Review Mode pauses at milestones for user approval but does not explicitly produce a reviewable plan before any edits occur. The capability requires producing a plan first and then executing — this is a checkpoint/approval mechanism, not a plan-then-execute mode.

Always Proceed Mode means the agent never pauses and executes changes immediately, but it does not describe setting a verifiable 'done' condition and looping autonomously until that condition is met. It is an execution-style setting, not a goal-directed iteration mechanism.
Worktree Support has agents operating in dedicated Git worktrees to isolate parallel work within a project, directly realizing the capability of parallel isolated workspaces without collisions.
Background Tasks explicitly run tracked, long-running operations asynchronously without blocking the primary agent, directly matching the capability.
Scheduled Tasks allow users to schedule messages to agents with repeatable time-based triggers, directly realizing the capability of scheduled/triggered runs.
Terminal Sandboxing provides kernel-level/native OS isolation for terminal commands executed by the agent, directly realizing sandboxed execution to limit blast radius.

Fine-Grained Permissions Engine represents every sensitive agent operation as a permission resource in a unified engine, directly realizing the capability of approving/denying what the agent is allowed to do.
Independent Network Access Control explicitly controls network connectivity for sandboxed commands independently, directly realizing the capability of restricting the agent's outbound network access.
Inspect Hooks are read-only, non-blocking hooks explicitly for logging, audit trails, and metrics collection, directly realizing the audit logging/telemetry capability.

Observability tracks per-turn and cumulative token usage and provides thinking traces, but does not mention cost tracking or the ability to cap token spend. Tracking usage is close, but capping/cost management is absent from the description, making this only a partial match — and partial matches do not count.
